macro_events.event_summary: Oil prices rising as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed amid Iran war concerns, threatening ~20% of global seaborne crude flows.
primary_drivers: Supply disruption to ~20% of global seaborne oil transiting the Strait of Hormuz
Geopolitical risk premium embedded in crude futures
Shift in shipping/freight routing and insurance costs

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with significant non-Gulf upstream production (Permian, Guyana, offshore) — realizes higher crude prices without direct Hormuz transit exposure. Every $10/bbl move ≈ ~$4B annual EBITDA uplift.
Similar to XOM: large Permian/US and Kazakhstan (Tengiz via CPC, not Hormuz) production benefits from price spike. Chevron's Gulf exposure is limited relative to peers.
Pure-play Permian producer — 100% US onshore, no Middle East exposure, high leverage to WTI. Among highest beta to crude spikes in large-cap E&P.
Frontline tanker rates rise materially on Hormuz closure — ton-mile demand surges as crude reroutes from alternative suppliers, war-risk insurance premiums lift spot rates.
Cheniere — US Gulf Coast LNG exporter becomes premium-priced alternative as Qatari LNG (~20% of global supply, all transits Hormuz) is stranded. Spot cargo margins widen.
Defense prime with direct exposure to Middle East conflict escalation — missile defense (THAAD, PAC-3), increased FMS orders from Gulf allies, replenishment demand.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25-30% of airline operating costs. Delta also operates its own Trainer refinery but net exposure to crude spike is strongly negative; limited fuel hedging industry-wide post-2022.
Unhedged jet fuel exposure; international routes via Middle East (especially to India/Asia) face rerouting costs and airspace closures compounding the fuel hit.
Southwest runs all-domestic but has historically thin margins and minimal hedges remaining — fuel spike compresses earnings directly.
Cruise operator with high bunker fuel sensitivity; Middle East itineraries (Dubai, Red Sea) disrupted, consumer discretionary also pressured by gasoline prices.
Petrochemical producer — naphtha/ethane feedstock costs spike with crude, while demand destruction from broader macro slowdown compresses spreads.
Consumer staples retailer faces margin compression as freight/diesel costs rise and lower-income consumer wallet share is eaten by gasoline prices at the pump.
Air freight has direct jet fuel exposure; fuel surcharges lag cost inflation and Middle East hub (Dubai for FedEx Asia-Europe) disruption adds routing costs.

headlines: Oil rises as Iran war worries keep markets on edge as Strait of Hormuz stays shut

macro_events.event_summary: Japan's core CPI reaccelerated in March after five months of easing, driven by energy cost pressures from the Iran war, raising pressure on the BOJ to hike rates.
primary_drivers: Higher crude/LNG prices from Middle East conflict feeding into Japan's import-heavy energy basket
Increased BOJ rate-hike probability → higher JGB yields, stronger yen
Global risk-off and safe-haven bid tied to Iran war escalation

beneficiaries.rationale: Mizuho ADR — Japanese megabank NIM directly expands as BOJ hike odds rise; higher JGB yields lift securities book carry and lending spreads. Most rate-sensitive liquid Japan bank ADR.
Mitsubishi UFJ ADR — largest Japanese bank; direct beneficiary of hawkish BOJ repricing; also has Morgan Stanley stake providing US optionality.
Energy supermajor — Iran war premium in Brent is the proximate cause cited; integrated upstream leverage to sustained $90+ crude.
Cheniere — Japan is a top LNG buyer; Middle East supply risk to Qatari/UAE cargoes and Strait of Hormuz transit boosts US LNG contract economics and spot JKM premium.

losers.rationale: Toyota ADR — stronger yen from BOJ hike expectations compresses translated overseas earnings; every ¥1 JPY/USD move is material to operating profit. Also faces higher domestic energy input costs.
Honda ADR — same yen-translation headwind as Toyota; exporter model directly penalized by BOJ hawkish shift.

headlines: Japan core inflation accelerates after five months as Iran war stokes energy worries
